# Setup: The Platform-Specific Zone

Every other chapter in this book teaches a portable baseline:
commands and habits that behave the same, in the common cases,
whether you are on a Mac, inside WSL2 on Windows, or SSH'd into
a Linux server. This chapter is the deliberate exception. Setup
cannot be portable, because the whole point of it is to install
the platform-specific things the rest of the book then treats as
given. Pretending otherwise would strand a beginner on line one.
So this is the one place platform-specifics are allowed to run
free: parallel tracks for macOS, Linux, and WSL2, each said
plainly, with the divergences flagged rather than smoothed over.

By the end you will have opened a terminal on your platform,
identified which shell you are running and why it matters,
learned how to install software through a package manager instead
of by downloading mystery binaries, and met the supply-chain
rule that governs the rest: verify before you run. Windows
readers also get the two things they specifically need, why to
work inside WSL2 and what will bite them if they try to follow
this book's instructions in PowerShell instead.

A note on where the output comes from, because this chapter's
provenance is unusually mixed. The Linux track is real output
from this book's Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04.5, GNU bash 5.1.16;
the `ch03-linux-*.txt` transcripts). The macOS facts are real,
captured read-only on the author's Mac by
`transcripts/capture-ch03-mac.sh` (the `ch03-*-mac.txt`
transcripts). Three kinds of thing are **documented and
quarantined** rather than run, each labeled where it appears,
version-stamped "current as of 2026-07-01," and pinned to
official documentation in `verification/chapter-03.md`: the WSL2
setup steps (no Windows machine exists to run them here), the
`apt` install commands (the sandbox has no root, so it cannot
install), and the PowerShell sidebar. One rule holds across all
three tracks and is the reason the quarantine is so large: this
chapter shows the real output of *inspection* commands, but it
never runs an *installer*, on any machine, for exactly the reason
Chapter 7 would not run a blind `curl | sh`. One deliberate mask,
as in Chapter 2: where a Mac path would show the account name it
reads `/Users/[account]`, a scrub for this public book; the
brackets are a placeholder your own machine fills in.

## Opening a terminal, and knowing what you are in {.tier-beginner}

Chapter 2 drew the distinction this section rests on: the
terminal is the window, and the shell is the program running
inside it that actually reads your commands. To follow this book
you need to get a shell open, and how you do that is one of the
few genuinely platform-specific steps.

On a **Mac**, the terminal is an application called Terminal,
in `Applications/Utilities`; the fastest way to it is Spotlight
(Command-Space, type "Terminal"). Many researchers later switch
to iTerm2, a free alternative with more features, but the
built-in Terminal is enough for everything here. Either one opens
a shell prompt, and on a modern Mac that shell is zsh.

On **Linux**, if you are working at a desktop the terminal is a
built-in application (GNOME Terminal, Konsole, and others, opened
from the applications menu). More often, as a researcher, you
reach a Linux shell by connecting to a server over SSH, which
Chapter 14 will cover; when you do, you land at a shell prompt
directly with no window of your own to open. Either way the
default shell is almost always bash.

On **Windows**, the short version is: do not use the terminals
Windows gives you by default (PowerShell and Command Prompt) for
this book's work. Install WSL2, the Windows Subsystem for Linux,
which runs a real Linux distribution inside Windows, and work in
*its* shell instead. Installing it is a single command, run once
in an Administrator PowerShell window, and it is the one time you
will touch PowerShell: you use it to escape to Linux (Microsoft
Learn, "How to install Linux on Windows with WSL," current as of
2026-07-01):

```text
# Run ONCE in an ADMINISTRATOR PowerShell, then reboot.
# Installs WSL2 with Ubuntu by default. Not run here.
wsl --install
```

After it finishes and you reboot, you launch the Ubuntu shell
from the Start menu or, better, from Windows Terminal, and you
get a real bash prompt on a real Linux system. Everything this
book does then applies to you the same as to a Linux reader, with
the divergences flagged. The rest of this chapter's Windows
guidance assumes you are inside that WSL2 shell; the reasons to
prefer it over PowerShell are the sidebar at the end.

The terminal application itself, Terminal versus iTerm2 versus
Windows Terminal, is mostly cosmetic: tabs, colors, fonts, split
panes. It is worth almost none of your attention right now. What
runs inside it, the shell, is the thing the next section pins
down.

## Which shell you have, and why it matters {.tier-beginner}

Chapter 2 promised that this chapter would sort out which shell
you have. Two commands answer it. `echo $SHELL` prints your
**login shell**, the one set as your default; `ps -p $$` reports
the shell **running right now**, which is usually but not always
the same. On the author's Mac:

```bash
$ echo $SHELL
/bin/zsh
```

The login shell is zsh, which is the macOS default and prints a
prompt ending in `%`. In the book's Linux sandbox the running
shell is bash, whose prompt ends in `$`:

```bash
$ ps -p $$ -o comm=; true
bash
```

```bash
$ bash --version | head -1
GNU bash, version 5.1.16(1)-release (aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu)
```

(The trailing `; true` is a capture detail: with `ps` as the
only command, bash would replace itself with `ps` and report
`ps`; the extra command keeps `$$` naming the shell. At your own
prompt you type just `ps -p $$`.) That `$`-versus-`%` split is
the small difference Chapter 1 told you to notice: macOS defaults
to zsh, while most Linux systems and the WSL2 Ubuntu shell
default to bash. The two are close relatives, and for interactive
use the difference rarely matters. It matters for *scripts*, and
this book takes a firm position that Chapter 11 will act on: write
scripts for bash, with portable habits, never in zsh-only syntax.
zsh is fine at the prompt; it is not the target you write
automation against.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
There is a sharper shell divergence hiding on the Mac, and it is
about *version*, not name. macOS ships a system bash frozen at
version 3.2.57, released in 2007, because later bash is licensed
under the GPLv3, which Apple declines to ship; the author's
`/bin/bash --version` confirms 3.2.57 (current as of 2026-07-01,
`transcripts/ch03-shell-mac.txt`). Linux and the WSL2 Ubuntu
shell run bash 5.x (the sandbox's 5.1.16 above). A script that
uses a bash-4-or-later feature, associative arrays are the usual
one, runs fine on Linux and fails on a Mac's `/bin/bash`. This is
the macOS bash-3.2 floor, and Chapter 11 returns to it when it
sets the scripting target; the fix is to install a modern bash
through Homebrew (below) and point your scripts at that, because
the *system* `/bin/bash` will stay at 3.2 no matter what you
install. For now, just know which bash your platform hands you by
default.
:::

## Package managers: how you install things {.tier-beginner}

The wrong way to install research software is to hunt down a
website, download a binary, and hope. The right way is a
**package manager**: one tool that installs, updates, and removes
software and its dependencies from a curated source, so you can
say what you want and let it handle the rest. Which package
manager you use is platform-specific, which is exactly why it
lives in this chapter.

On **macOS** the de facto standard is **Homebrew**, driven by the
`brew` command. It is not preinstalled; installing it safely is
the next section. Once it is in place, `brew install <name>`
fetches and installs a tool, and on Apple Silicon everything
lands under the prefix `/opt/homebrew` (the section after next
returns to why that matters for your PATH).

On **Linux**, and therefore inside the WSL2 Ubuntu shell too, the
Debian/Ubuntu family uses **APT**, driven by `apt`, which sits on
top of the lower-level `dpkg`. Both are preinstalled; you can
confirm they are there without installing anything:

```bash
$ apt --version
apt 2.4.14 (arm64)

$ dpkg --version | head -1
Debian 'dpkg' package management program version 1.21.1 (arm64).
```

Installing a package with APT needs administrator rights, so the
command is prefixed with `sudo`, and it comes in two steps: update
the local index of what is available, then install. The book's
sandbox has no root, so it cannot run these, but they are the
standard Debian/Ubuntu form (Ubuntu Server documentation,
"Install and manage packages," current as of 2026-07-01):

```text
# Debian/Ubuntu, incl. the WSL2 Ubuntu shell; needs root.
# Shown, not run here (this book's sandbox has no root).
sudo apt update
sudo apt install tldr
```

Other Linux families differ: Fedora and RHEL use `dnf`, Arch uses
`pacman`. This book does not teach them, but the shape is the
same, and you route to your distribution's manager the way you
route to your platform's. (Ubuntu's own docs note a wrinkle worth
carrying to Chapter 11: `apt` is meant for interactive use, and
`apt-get` is the stable interface to call from scripts.)

That `tldr` above is not a random example. Chapter 2 introduced
the `tldr` help tool and said its install was a separate step
this chapter would handle: here it is. On macOS the recipe is
`brew install tealdeer` (`tealdeer` is a fast `tldr` client); on
Debian/Ubuntu it is the `sudo apt install tldr` shown above. The
author's Mac runs tealdeer 1.8.1 (current as of 2026-07-01,
`transcripts/ch03-toolchain-mac.txt`), the client Chapter 2's
`tldr` output came from.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The install *command* is one of the least portable things in the
whole book: `brew install X` on a Mac, `sudo apt install X` on
Debian/Ubuntu, `dnf`/`pacman` elsewhere. That divergence is
precisely why setup is quarantined to this chapter. The payoff is
that once a tool is installed, the book can teach a common
baseline for it, with the remaining divergences flagged, which is
what lets the rest of the book stay largely platform-neutral.
Homebrew muddies
the line in a useful way: it also runs on Linux and WSL2 (under
`/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew`), so you *can* use `brew` on a server
to get a newer tool than the system `apt` offers. Use the system
manager for system things and reach for Homebrew when you need a
version the distribution does not carry.
:::

## The Apple Silicon PATH note {.tier-beginner}

Chapter 2's PATH divergence showed that on an Apple Silicon Mac,
Homebrew's tools sit at the front of your PATH under
`/opt/homebrew/bin`. This section is the mechanism behind that,
because it is the piece a new Mac setup most often gets wrong.
Homebrew's prefix is where it puts everything:

```bash
$ command -v brew
/opt/homebrew/bin/brew
```

```bash
$ brew --prefix
/opt/homebrew
```

Installing a tool drops its executable in `/opt/homebrew/bin`, but
that does nothing until that directory is on your PATH, and ahead
of the system directories so a brewed tool beats an older system
copy of the same name (Chapter 2's first-match-wins rule).
Homebrew arranges this with one line its installer tells you to
add to your shell configuration,
`eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"`. Note the full prefix
path: on a fresh install `brew` is not on your PATH yet, so you
cannot rely on the bare `brew` name until this very line has run,
which is why Homebrew's docs spell out the prefix. It sets up
Homebrew's environment so that `/opt/homebrew/bin` sits at the
front of your PATH for every new shell (Homebrew documentation,
"Installation," current as of 2026-07-01; Chapter 2's captured
login PATH began exactly `/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin`,
ahead of the system directories). You do
not need to understand the startup-file machinery yet, only that
this line is what connects "I installed it" to "I can run it";
where that line lives, and the login-versus-interactive shell
rules that decide when it runs, is Chapter 18's subject.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The most common setup failure is a tool that installed cleanly but
the shell cannot find: you type its name and get `command not
found`. It almost always means the tool's directory is not on your
PATH. On a Mac it is usually the
`eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"` line missing from your
shell config, so `/opt/homebrew/bin` was never
added; with tools that install into `~/.local/bin` (many Python
and Rust tools do) it is that directory missing from PATH. The
diagnosis is Chapter 2's `command -v <name>`, which tells you
whether the shell can find it at all; the fix is to put the right
directory on your PATH, which Chapter 18 makes stick across
new shells. Add only directories you trust and mind their order:
an attacker-controlled directory early on PATH is the
PATH-hijacking risk Chapter 7 raised, and it applies to anything
you prepend here.
:::

## Installing safely: verify, do not blind-pipe {.tier-beginner}

Homebrew has to be installed before you can use it, and the way
its own homepage tells you to do that is, uncomfortably, the exact
pattern Chapter 7 spent a whole section refusing to run (Homebrew
homepage, current as of 2026-07-01, shown wrapped to fit; it is a
single line there):

```text
# The official Homebrew installer. NOT run here.
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL \
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/\
install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

This downloads a script and pipes it straight into `bash`, which
runs it as you, with your permissions. Chapter 7's rule was blunt:
never run code you have not read, fetched over a network you do
not control. Homebrew is not an anonymous stranger, and that is
the honest nuance here. It is a well-known project, the download
is over HTTPS from an official location, and its installer is a
careful script that prints exactly what it will do and pauses for
your confirmation before doing it (its own docs say so). That is
why many careful people run it as printed. But the *mechanism* is
identical to the one that is dangerous in general, so the maximally
safe path, and the one this book teaches, is Chapter 7's: separate
downloading from running, so there is a moment in between to look.

```text
# fetch, read, then run (never pipe straight to a shell):
curl -fsSL -o install.sh https://example.com/install.sh
less install.sh              # read the whole thing
shasum -a 256 install.sh     # match the published hash
/bin/bash install.sh         # only after the first three
```

That third step, the checksum, is how you verify a download was
not tampered with or swapped in transit. You compute a hash of the
file and compare it to the hash the project publishes. The same
bytes always produce the same hash, on any machine, which is the
whole point. In the Linux sandbox:

```bash
$ printf 'hello\n' | sha256sum
5891b5b522d5df086d0ff0b110fbd9d21bb4fc7163af34d08286a2e846f6be03  -
```

and on the author's Mac the identical bytes give the identical
digest, from a differently named tool:

```bash
$ printf 'hello\n' | shasum -a 256
5891b5b522d5df086d0ff0b110fbd9d21bb4fc7163af34d08286a2e846f6be03  -
```

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The checksum tool has different names on the two platforms, which
trips people following cross-platform instructions. Linux (and the
WSL2 Ubuntu shell) ships GNU `sha256sum`; macOS does not, and
gives you `shasum -a 256` instead (a Perl tool, where `-a 256`
picks the SHA-256 algorithm). As the two blocks above show, they
produce byte-identical digests, so a hash a project publishes
verifies the same either way; only the command you type to compute
it differs. On a Mac reach for `shasum -a 256`, on Linux for
`sha256sum`, and Chapter 12's `make check` uses this same hashing
to lock the running example's data.
:::

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
Piping a fetched script straight into a shell, `curl ... | bash`,
including the Homebrew one-liner above, runs code you have not read
with your full user privileges: free to modify your shell startup
files, your SSH keys, or your PATH. A tampered or
man-in-the-middled download runs just the same, and the timing
side channel Chapter 7 described means a server can even serve
clean code to anyone who saves the file and malicious code to
anyone piping it. Homebrew earns a measure of trust through its
official HTTPS source and a script that shows its plan and pauses,
which is why running it as printed is a defensible choice, not a
reckless one. But the safe habit generalizes and its exceptions do
not: prefer fetch-read-run, take the expected checksum from a
*different* channel than the one that served the download (never
from the same page), and never pipe an *unfamiliar* project's
installer into a shell at all. Chapter 7 owns the mechanics of
this hazard, and Chapter 17 returns to it because an AI agent can
compose and run exactly this line on your behalf.
:::

## /mnt/c and the WSL2 boundary {.tier-advanced}

Chapter 2 noted that inside WSL2 you work in one Unix tree with
the Windows `C:` drive mounted at `/mnt/c`, and promised this
chapter would say what that means in practice. The practical
guidance is a single rule with real consequences: **keep your
project inside the Linux home directory (`~`), not under
`/mnt/c`.** (This section is documented against Microsoft's docs,
not run, because no Windows machine exists in this book's build;
current as of 2026-07-01.)

Two reasons. First, speed: files in the Linux filesystem are far
faster for Linux tools to read and write than files reached across
the `/mnt/c` boundary, and a data pipeline that touches many files
feels the difference sharply; Microsoft's own guidance is to store
project files in the Linux filesystem when you work on them with
Linux tools (Microsoft Learn, "Working across Windows and Linux
file systems"). Second, correctness: the Windows drive carries
Windows conventions, including CRLF line endings on files created
by Windows editors, which can quietly break shell scripts and
throw off the checksums this chapter just taught. `/mnt/c` is
there for when you genuinely need to reach a Windows file; it is
not where your repository should live. The line-ending issue
returns in Chapter 11, which will pin the locale and text settings
that keep results stable across machines.

## The PowerShell sidebar {.tier-advanced}

This book does not teach PowerShell. Windows researchers should
work inside WSL2, where the entire book applies. But you will meet
PowerShell, it is what opens by default on Windows, so here is what
bites if you try to run this book's Unix instructions in it. Four
points, then we stop (all current as of 2026-07-01, pinned to
Microsoft Learn).

First, why WSL2 and not PowerShell: this book's tools, examples,
and habits are Unix. WSL2 gives you a real Linux shell where they
work unchanged; PowerShell is a different shell with a different
model, so following Unix instructions in it means constant
translation and surprising failures. Use WSL2 and skip the
translation.

Second, the deepest difference: a PowerShell pipeline passes
**objects**, not text. Where a Unix pipe sends a stream of
characters from one command to the next, PowerShell's `|` sends
structured .NET objects between cmdlets, so `Get-Process | Stop-Process`
hands an actual process object along, not its printed name
(Microsoft Learn, `about_Pipelines`). The Unix mental model of
text flowing through `grep`, `cut`, and `sed` does not transfer,
because there is no text stream to filter.

Third, the fake-alias trap. PowerShell defines aliases that look
like Unix commands but are not them: `ls` and `cat` are aliases
for the cmdlets `Get-ChildItem` and `Get-Content`
(Microsoft Learn, `about_Aliases`), so the names work but the
options you know do not. Worse for this book's supply-chain
theme, Windows PowerShell 5.1, the version preinstalled on
Windows, aliased `curl` and `wget` to its `Invoke-WebRequest`
cmdlet, which behaves nothing like the real tools; PowerShell 7
removed those two aliases. Either way, do not assume `curl` or
`wget` at a PowerShell prompt is the Unix tool you meant.

Fourth, execution policy and path separators. PowerShell has an
**execution policy** that can refuse to run scripts; the default
on Windows clients is `RemoteSigned`, which blocks unsigned
scripts downloaded from the internet until you unblock or sign
them (Microsoft Learn, `about_Execution_Policies`). And Windows
paths use backslashes with a semicolon PATH separator (`C:\...`,
`;`), against Unix forward slashes and colons (`/...`, `:`), so
paths copied between the two do not just work. That is the
sidebar; for everything else, be in WSL2.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These set up and check the environment the asset-pricing example
needs. Most only inspect and destroy nothing; the last one
installs `tldr`, a small helper worth keeping.

1. Identify your shell. Run `echo $SHELL` and `ps -p $$`, and note
   whether your prompt ends in `$` or `%`. Say in one sentence
   which shell each command reported and whether they agree, then
   predict what a friend on the other platform would see.

2. Find your package manager. On a Mac run `brew --version`; on
   Linux or WSL2 run `apt --version`. Then confirm the tools this
   book leans on are present with `command -v git` and, for
   checksums, `command -v shasum` (Mac) or `command -v sha256sum`
   (Linux). Note anything missing; it is what you would install
   next.

3. On a Mac, inspect your PATH. Run `brew --prefix` and confirm it
   is `/opt/homebrew`, then run `echo $PATH` and check that
   `/opt/homebrew/bin` appears before `/usr/bin`. Explain, using
   Chapter 2's first-match-wins rule, why that order is what lets a
   brewed tool win over the system copy.

4. Practice verify-before-run. Pick a real installer that
   publishes a `curl`-into-shell one-liner (Homebrew's will do).
   Without running it, fetch the script with `curl -fsSL -o
   install.sh <url>`, read it with `less install.sh`, and find the
   lines where it writes to your PATH or shell startup files.
   Decide whether you would have run it blind.

5. Confirm the pipeline's Python half runs. `cd` into the
   asset-pricing project and run `python3 --version` and, if you
   use it, `uv --version`. If `uv` is missing, name the command
   you would use to install it on your platform; the operational
   use of uv to pin the environment is Chapter 13's subject, not
   this chapter's.

6. Install `tldr` and cash in Chapter 2's promise. Use your
   package manager (`brew install tealdeer`, or `sudo apt install
   tldr`), then run `tldr tar` and compare it to the output
   Chapter 2 showed.
